pulls.title: Add Mission-Bound Runtime Enforcement companion draft
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Brings the runtime companion (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime`) into this repo alongside the issuance MVP and wires it into the build and README.

## Changes

- **`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime.md`** (new) — copied from the `mission-bound-authorization` suite, with `venue.github`/`venue.latest` repointed to `mcguinness/draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`. Its only cross-reference is to the MVP draft already in this repo (`I-D.draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`).
- **`.gitignore`** — ignore the generated `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime.xml` artifact.
- **`README.md`** — converted to the standard i-d-template multi-draft layout, adding the runtime draft's Editor's Copy / Datatracker / Individual Draft / diff links. `regenerate: on` preserved.

The build auto-discovers `draft-*.md`, so no Makefile change was needed.

## Verification

Built both drafts to text via the i-d-template toolchain — `kramdown-rfc` and `xml2rfc-txt` both pass `OK`. Remaining warnings are benign (RFC2119/8174 inline + YAML header; explicit reference block for the not-yet-published MVP draft).

pulls.title: feat: Non-goals section + standardized consumption-bound vocabulary
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Two changes, both pre-empting recurring agent-authorization critiques without altering the core normative model.

**1. Non-goals subsection (legibility, no normative change).** Names the deliberate deferrals in implementers' own vocabulary, and routes each to where it belongs:
- Semantic / intent verification -> the runtime layer (`{{runtime-boundary}}`); reasoning-declaration attestation is outside both layers.
- Just-in-time Mission expansion -> future (widening needs a successor Mission).
- Lifecycle event distribution -> a Shared Signals (RFC 8935) / CAEP profile, future.
- Human-in-the-loop suspension -> a `suspended` state + holding-token resume, future lifecycle work.
- Multi-hop cross-domain provenance -> future (single hop only here).
- Decentralized agent identity -> out of scope (klrc / WIMSE); this profile governs the approved-task artifact, not the identity.

**2. Standardized consumption-bound vocabulary.** Define `max_budget { amount, currency }`, `max_calls [ { scope, count } ]`, and `max_duration` (RFC 3339 duration) as recognized Mission-level `context` members, so issuance and the runtime layer share one key vocabulary. They are carried and committed by `proposal_hash`, but — unlike `max_derivations` (AS-enforced) — they are **consumption bounds enforced at the point of use by the runtime layer** (`{{runtime-boundary}}`); absent that layer they are not enforced. Added RFC 8935 informative reference. The runtime draft's metering section is updated separately to name these keys.

Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0 / dup-ref 0.

pulls.title: feat(runtime): name standardized consumption-bound keys in metering
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Companion to the issuance-draft consumption-vocabulary change (PR #2). The runtime metering section now names the three Mission-level consumption bounds the issuance profile defines, with per-key enforcement semantics:

- `max_budget` — atomic reserve-or-charge against remaining balance; refuse when insufficient.
- `max_calls` — atomic per-`scope` counter; refuse past `count`.
- `max_duration` — elapsed wall-clock since Mission activation; refuse past the bound.

Per-entry `constraints` expressing a consumption bound are metered the same way. Issuance and runtime now share one key vocabulary. Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0.

pulls.title: Align TOC with OAuth RFC naming and conventions
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Structural review of the draft's table of contents, aligning section names and ordering with OAuth/IETF RFC conventions. **Heading text and structure only — no normative content changes.** All cross-reference anchors are preserved, so no `{{...}}` links break.

## Tier 1 — naming alignment

- **Title Case** all section/subsection titles per OAuth/IETF house style (RFC 6749, 9396, 9068, 9700); identifiers stay lowercase in backticks (e.g. `The mission Claim`, `mission_id Format`), mirroring RFC 9396's own `Request Parameter "authorization_details"`.
- **IANA subsections** renamed to the exact registry titles used by RFC 9396 §14:
  - `OAuth parameter` → **OAuth Parameters Registration**
  - `JWT claim` → **JSON Web Token Claims Registration**
  - `OAuth token introspection response member` → **OAuth Token Introspection Response Registration**
  - `OAuth Authorization Server metadata` → **OAuth Authorization Server Metadata Registration**
- `Discovery` → **Authorization Server Metadata** (RFC 8414 / RFC 9396 §10).
- `Modeling tools` → **Modeling Tools and Function Calls** (the old name read as software modeling).

## Tier 2 — structure / ordering

- Merge the BCP-14 requirements language with the term definitions into **§1.7 Conventions and Terminology**, kept ahead of §1.8 Conformance (so the keywords are defined before Conformance uses them).
- Move **Principal Model** up to **Overview §2.1**, before any section relies on the `(iss, sub)` model — and no longer adjacent to Terminology, removing role-definition overlap.
- Fold **Subset Rule** under **Authorization Derivation** as a subsection, where narrowing is first defined.
- Move **Authorization Server Metadata** to **after the protocol mechanics, before Security Considerations**, matching RFC 9396 ordering.

## Verification

- Builds clean: `kramdown-rfc` + `xml2rfc-txt` both `OK`.
- Zero broken cross-references in the rendered output.
- Directional prose ("above/below") audited near every moved section — none went stale; one ("the rules above … subset …") is now *corrected* by the Subset Rule move.
- Confirmed in rendered text that §1.7 (requirements language) precedes §1.8 Conformance.

pulls.title: Refine section names to match blog and draft vocabulary
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Heading-text refinements for a consistent **Mission Intent → Mission Authority → Mission Approval → Mission Record** progression (proposal → derived authority → approval → resulting record). Anchors and cross-references unchanged; build clean, no broken refs.

- **Authorization Derivation → Mission Authority** — the section's output is the **Authority Set** committed as `authority_hash`; "authority" is the draft's distinctive noun (blog: "derive the candidate authority"). Anchor `{#authorization-derivation}` preserved.
- **The Approval Event → Mission Approval** — matches the draft's own "the Mission is the approval" and the blog's "approved mission". The "approval event" concept term stays in the body. Anchor `{#approval-event}` preserved.
- **The Mission → Mission Record** — matches the section's own opening ("A Mission is the durable record created at the approval event") and the existing `{#mission-record}` anchor. Anchor preserved.

pulls.title: Resolve review feedback: duration citation, purpose semantics, derivation invariant
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses the clear-cut items from review feedback. The remaining normative forks (sender-constraint level, conformance profile split, consent_rendering_hash) need an explicit decision and will land separately.

## Fixes

- **`max_duration` citation (issuance + runtime drafts).** It was described as an "RFC 3339 duration," but RFC 3339's normative body is date-time only; the duration grammar (`PT8H`) is ISO 8601, captured non-normatively in RFC 3339 Appendix A. Now: "an ISO 8601 duration (e.g. `PT8H`), matching the `duration` rule in Appendix A of [RFC3339]." Fixed in both drafts for consistency.
- **`purpose` policy/audit contradiction.** It said "for policy and audit" yet "MUST NOT be used to derive, widen, or gate authority." Now recorded for disclosure and audit, opaque to derivation; a deployment MAY consult it for *out-of-band* policy that does not affect derived authority.
- **Derivation invariant restated in Mission Authority.** Added the invariant where implementers need it: the derived **Authority Set** (not the Intent text) is what the Approver consents to and what carries authority; Intent members constrain what the AS MAY derive, never widen it.
- **`mission_resource_access.resource` reframed (item 7), not renamed.** The wire field stays `resource`. The confusing part was its *negative* definition ("not the RFC 8707 param, not `locations`, MUST NOT treat as either"). It is now defined positively: it is a protected-resource identifier of the same kind as the RFC 8707 `resource` value (RFC 9728), from which the AS derives the token `aud`, carried per `authorization_details` entry (which RFC 9396 permits) rather than as the request parameter. The RFC 9396 orthogonality and the distinction from `locations` are kept as clarifications. The value's alignment with RFC 8707 is intentional and now stated, not disclaimed.

## Also verified (no change needed)

- **Section-reference drift:** not reproducible. The source has 0 unresolved refs; all `Section N` literals are correct external-RFC citations; `{{runtime-boundary}}` renders as §14.3 consistently, and the published editor's copy is current. The reviewer reviewed a stale snapshot (Security at §16).

Both drafts build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), no broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Resolve review items 5 and 8 (compatibility-aware)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the remaining review forks in their compatibility-aware form (option 2). Item 6 (sender-constraint) is intentionally unchanged.

## Item 8 — Conformance: named optional capabilities

Keeps the two mandatory roles (Mission Issuer, Mission-Aware RS) but turns the loosely-described optional features into three named, independently claimable capabilities — **Delegation**, **Introspection**, **Cross-Domain** — so an implementation can state exactly what it supports (e.g. "Mission Issuer with Delegation and Cross-Domain"). No separate profile sections; each capability's own section still holds its detailed requirements.

## Item 5 — Consent rendering: out-of-band SHOULD for high-risk

Adds a **SHOULD** that deployments whose Missions carry high-risk authority record presentation-level audit evidence **out of band** (e.g. a hash over the exact rendered consent disclosure), and names the threat the reviewer raised: a buggy/malicious rendering layer can mislead the Approver with no committed trace. The on-the-wire `consent_rendering_hash` and its structured disclosure object remain **deferred to future work** — a normative wire SHOULD isn't possible without defining that object, which would expand MVP scope.

## Item 6 — Sender-constraint: unchanged

Primary tokens stay **SHOULD** sender-constrained; refresh, delegated, and cross-domain tokens remain **MUST**. (Compatibility-aware choice; the security-optimal alternative would make all Mission-bound tokens MUST.)

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), no broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Add non-normative freshness-posture guidance table (runtime)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds an illustrative table to the runtime draft's *Mission state and freshness* section, mapping each action class to a suggested freshness posture.

## What

| Class | Suggested freshness posture |
|---|---|
| Consequential read | Token lifetime or a short state lease; tighter for privacy-sensitive/cross-tenant/bulk |
| Consequential write | A short state lease, typically minutes |
| Irreversible action | Immediate check or single-use permit |
| External commitment | Immediate check or single-use permit |
| Privileged administration | Immediate check, suitable for composition with local step-up |

## Why it's safe

- **Non-normative.** It illustrates the existing normative requirement (each deployment MUST publish its maximum staleness bound per action class) without imposing universal values.
- **Consistent taxonomy.** The five action classes match the *Action classification* table verbatim — no drift.
- **Established format.** Same kramdown GFM table syntax already used by the classification table.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: feat: OAuth Extensions + AuthZEN Profile (recast from the suite, building on the MVPs)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Adds two thin profiles that build on the two MVPs, recast from the `mission-bound-authorization` suite essays so the OAuth/runtime mechanics live in exactly one place (the MVPs).

**`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-extensions` — "Mission-Bound Authorization for OAuth 2.0: Extensions"**
Recast of the suite "Mission-Bound OAuth Profile". Keeps only the OPTIONAL OAuth-substrate capabilities the issuance MVP defers: a canonical by-`mission_id` Mission Status operation, a lifecycle management endpoint (`suspend`/`resume`/`complete`), graduated revocation-enforcement classes, RFC 9701 signed status evidence, and a Common Constraints registry. Normative dependency on `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`; **re-specifies none** of its mechanics (intent submission, derivation, the `mission` claim, code redemption, token exchange, ID-JAG, lifecycle gating, sender-constraint are all referenced, not duplicated).

**`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-authzen` — "Mission-Bound Runtime Enforcement: AuthZEN Profile"**
Recast of the suite "Mission-Bound Runtime Enforcement Profile". The runtime MVP says AuthZEN is recommended but is *not* an AuthZEN profile; this **is** that profile: Mission-to-policy materialization, the concrete AuthZEN PDP-request envelope (`context.mission`/`actor`/`parameters`/`audience`/`freshness`), and the Decision/Execution Evidence Object schemas. Normative dependency on `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-runtime` and AUTHZEN; defers action classification, PEP placement, parameter binding, metering, and enforcement scope to the MVP.

Both build clean (errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0). The README draft list is auto-regenerated by the i-d-template CI. Suite-side cleanup (removing the originals + repointing references) follows separately in the suite repo.

pulls.title: refactor: focus the extensions draft to Mission Status/Lifecycle; Common Constraints to core
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Acts on the review note: each extension needs a clear scope and specific name (not a generic "extensions" grab-bag), and Common Constraints belongs in core.

**Renamed + refocused:** `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-extensions` -> **`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-status`** ("Mission Status and Lifecycle for OAuth 2.0"). Scope is now exactly the Mission state-management surfaces the issuance MVP defers: the by-`mission_id` Mission Status operation, the lifecycle management endpoint (`revoke`/`suspend`/`resume`/`complete`), graduated revocation-enforcement classes, and RFC 9701 signed status. Removed Common Constraints from it (section, the `mission_common_constraints_supported` metadata member, and its IANA registry).

**Common Constraints -> core** (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`): a new `Common Constraints` section defines registered-vs-deployment-defined keys with value/subset/intersection semantics and seeds `max_amount_usd`/`issued_after`/`issued_before`; the constraints member definition and the Subset Rule now reference it; and a new IANA "Mission Common Constraints" registry (Specification Required) is created in core.

Both drafts build clean (errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0). Note: this touches the issuance MVP core, which has active WIP on the `full-spec-review-consistency` branch — a rebase there may need to reconcile the new Common Constraints section.

pulls.title: docs(oauth-mission): consistency — runtime-enforcement-evidence term + scope reflow
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Two clean editorial/consistency edits to the issuance draft (the uncommitted WIP from the full-spec consistency pass):

- `per-action decision evidence (a policy-decision record per call)` -> `runtime enforcement evidence for each consequential action`, in the "Issuance scope, not runtime enforcement" list — aligns the term with the runtime draft (`decision evidence` -> `runtime enforcement evidence`). No semantic change; the bullet still states issuance does not produce it.
- Reflow of the "Scope and future work" paragraph into two paragraphs.

No normative change (no RFC 2119 keywords touched). Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0.

pulls.title: style: parameter/member definitions as OAuth-RFC definition lists
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Aligns request/response parameter, claim, metadata, and JSON-member definitions across the OAuth-substrate drafts with OAuth-RFC house style (RFC 6749/9126/9396): a kramdown-rfc **definition list** with the description leading with the BCP 14 optionality keyword and the type, instead of the bullet `` `name` (type, required): desc `` form.

```
`goal`:
: REQUIRED. A string. A human-readable statement of the task,
  for rendering to the Approver. Maximum 4096 characters.
```

- **issuance**: Mission Intent members (nested `context` -> `max_budget`/`max_calls`), `mission_resource_access` members, Mission record fields, the `mission` claim.
- **status**: Mission Status + lifecycle request members, AS metadata members.
- **authzen**: PDP-request `context` members, Decision/Execution Evidence Object members, capability source binding (nested `freshness`).
- **runtime**: no matching definition lists (its content is contract prose; Terminology already used the def-list form) — unchanged.

This intentionally introduces BCP 14 `REQUIRED`/`OPTIONAL`/`CONDITIONAL` markers (optionality of a parameter is normative, per OAuth convention). IANA templates, JSON examples, tables, and ordinary prose bullets were left alone. Keyword mapping is exact (required->REQUIRED, optional->OPTIONAL). All four drafts build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0.

pulls.title: rename: proposal_hash -> intent_hash
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: Renames the integrity-anchor field `proposal_hash` to `intent_hash` across the OAuth-substrate drafts. It commits the approved **Mission Intent** (the defined term) and parallels `authority_hash` (which commits the Authority Set) — `intent_hash` names the object it commits, and matches the anchor `typ`, which is already `mission-intent`. It is a record/audit anchor (not IANA-registered, not a token claim), so the rename is low-risk. The concept "the Intent is the proposal" remains in prose unchanged. Issuance, authzen, runtime build clean (0/0/0); status had no occurrences.

pulls.title: fix(oauth-mission-runtime): resolve OAuth expert review findings
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the complete OAuth expert review of the runtime draft (3 expert lenses). Build clean: errors 0 / long-lines 0 / unused-refs 0 / dup-ref 0.

**High**
- **H1 (WGLC blocker):** the evidence section is now one record type ("runtime enforcement evidence record") for decision and refusal paths, instead of implying a "decision evidence" vs "enforcement evidence" two-object split it elsewhere defers.
- **H2:** the `cnf`/proof-of-possession MUST now anchors to the issuance profiles PoP rules (`{{I-D.draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission}}`) rather than an unreferenced "profile in use" — RFC 7800/9449/8705 were never cited here, and deferring is cleaner than adding them. (The RFC 6750 "undefined reference" raised by one lens was a false alarm — verified the build is clean.)
- **H3:** irreversible actions, external commitments, and privileged administration now MUST use a **single-use decision identifier** (not a validity window alone), which a permit window did not bound — closing replay-within-window double-execution of unmetered irreversible actions.

**Medium**
- **M1** define "policy-view version" (and distinguish it from issuance `policy_version`). **M2** `max_duration` metered as cumulative activity (matches issuance), not "elapsed since activation". **M3** consumed single-use identifiers MUST be persisted + re-presentation refused fail-closed, independent of metering (+ failure-mode row). **M4** require an execution-outcome record keyed to the decision id for the irreversible classes, so decision and execution reconcile 1:1 (schema deferred). **M5** clarify `mission_expiry` is enforced transitively via the token `exp`.

**Low**
- **L1** client-instance identifier marked a deployment-defined correlator. **L2** classification-floor "minimum" sourced to Resource policy (was an untestable hook). **L4** reference hygiene (bare informative cites; drop RFC 2119/8174 from YAML). L3 (`intent_hash` "when known") was already adequately hedged.

The issuance/runtime boundary, `parameter_digest` reuse of issuance canonicalization, fail-closed failure modes, and distributed-metering honesty were verified sound and unchanged.

pulls.title: Review Mission Status draft: RFC alignment, fixes, and build wiring
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Full structural + editorial review of `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-status` against OAuth RFC conventions. The draft was already well-shaped, so this is targeted fixes — **no section reordering** (the five-capability layout, the "This section is OPTIONAL" openers, and metadata-after-mechanics already match convention).

## Correctness
- **IANA intro was wrong.** It claimed "OAuth AS metadata members, a media type, **and a new registry**" — no registry was ever defined. Corrected to no-new-registry, with a one-line note that a registry is the vehicle *if* future revisions want enforcement-class extensibility. (Kept the value spaces closed/inline — minimal.)
- **`private_key_jwt` cited RFC 7521** (the abstract assertion framework); private-key-JWT *client authentication* is **RFC 7523**. Fixed.
- **No TLS reference.** "TLS 1.2 or later" now cites **BCP 195 (RFC 9325)**.

## OAuth-RFC convention alignment
- Title-case **"Worked Request Example"** (was sentence case; siblings were Title Case).
- IANA subsections → **"… Registration"** ("OAuth Authorization Server Metadata Registration", "Media Type Registration"), matching RFC 9396 and the issuance profile.
- **"Conventions and Definitions" → "Conventions and Terminology"** for consistency with the issuance profile (note: "Definitions" is the i-d-template default and itself fine — this is sibling consistency, not an RFC fix).

## Structural / editorial
- **Cross-surface schema divergence (structural):** the dedicated Status Response carries `state`/`authority_hash`/`expires_at` at top level, while the introspection projection nests them under `mission`. Added a one-line justification — the projection must not reshape the MVP's existing nested `mission` member; the dedicated response is greenfield. (Documented, not re-nested.)
- Reworded the confusing "two non-terminal-and-terminal states."
- Clarified that the "Error Responses" table mixes success outcomes (`ok`/`terminated`/`suspended` → 200) with hard errors.
- Left the cross-document `Section "…"` references as-is (they track the issuance profile's current section names; fragile to rename but practical for a co-maintained I-D).

## Build wiring (the draft was added tracked but unwired)
- gitignore `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-status.xml`; add the draft to the README — same wiring the runtime companion got.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

## Conformance placement
Kept late (after Privacy): it references every extension defined above it, so it can't move earlier. The asymmetry with the issuance profile (early §1.8) is justified by content order.

pulls.title: Avoid awkward 'by-`mission_id`' / 'by-token' phrasing in Mission Status draft
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Editorial follow-up to the Mission Status review. The compound-adjective constructions `by-`mission_id`` and `by-token` read awkwardly (14 occurrences). Replaced with cleaner phrasing:

- **`by-token introspection` → `token introspection`** — the contrast is already clear from context.
- **`by-`mission_id` operation/surface` → `Mission Status operation/surface`**, with the keying expressed as a trailing phrase where it carries meaning (e.g. "a canonical Mission Status operation (keyed by `mission_id`)", "a lookup keyed by `mission_id`").

No normative change. Affected lines re-wrapped to the draft's ~70-col style. Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

Note: the issuance profile (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`) uses the same construction in ~7 places; left untouched here — happy to sweep it in a follow-up for cross-draft consistency if wanted.

pulls.title: Avoid awkward 'by-`mission_id`' / 'by-token' phrasing (issuance profile)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Cross-draft consistency follow-up to #16 (which de-awkwarded the Mission Status draft). Applies the same phrasing fix to the issuance profile's introspection and revocation discussion — 6 occurrences:

- `by-token introspection` → `token introspection`
- `canonical by-`mission_id` Mission Status surface` → `canonical Mission Status surface (keyed by `mission_id`)`
- `has no by-`mission_id` query to the origin` → `has no query to the origin keyed by `mission_id``

No normative change. Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references. Both drafts now use consistent, non-awkward phrasing.

pulls.title: Drop the undefined lifecycle_event response member
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Removes `lifecycle_event` from the Mission Lifecycle endpoint's worked example and explanatory text.

**Why:** it was shown on the wire but never defined (no member definition, no normative requirement, referenced by no Conformance/Security/IANA text), and it's redundant — it's returned only to the caller that just performed the operation, which already supplied/knows `operation`, `actor`, and `reason`. The party that would want an attributed transition record (an auditor) uses the canonical Status operation, which doesn't carry it. The response already confirms the outcome via `state` and the incremented `version`, and the AS records operation/actor/reason in its audit log.

Replaced the trailing sentence to make that explicit (audit log + state/version). No normative change. Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Relax introspection Mission projection signing (MUST -> SHOULD)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
The introspection Mission projection required an RFC 9701-signed response (MUST). That bar is too high:

- **Stricter than RFC 7662**, which protects equally-sensitive introspection data (`active`, `scope`) with TLS alone; RFC 9701 is an optional enhancement.
- **Stricter than the issuance profile it extends**, which permits the `mission` projection unsigned over TLS.
- **Unjustified for the topology**: token introspection is a direct, authenticated RS↔AS call over TLS.

**Change:** TLS is the baseline; the AS **SHOULD** return an RFC 9701-signed response where integrity must be verifiable independently of the transport (intermediaries, audit), advertised via `introspection_signing_alg_values_supported`. Updated for consistency across: intro bullet, projection requirement, conformance bullet, the "RFC 9701 vs. New Media Type" security note, and the metadata + privacy references.

**Unchanged:** the dedicated Mission Status operation still **MUST** be JWS-signed — its by-`mission_id` consumers (auditors, cross-domain Resource ASes) have no direct authenticated channel and need independently verifiable evidence.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Frame the Status nonce as a request challenge (clarity)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body: One-line reader-clarity note on the Mission Status `nonce`: it is a standard client challenge whose echo in the signed response anti-replay-binds the response to the specific request. No normative change. Builds clean, 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Harmonize Mission facts into one mission object shape
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Fixes the cross-surface schema divergence (the `mission` projection nesting fields vs. the dedicated Status Response carrying them top-level) by **aligning the shapes**, not documenting the difference.

**One `mission` object everywhere.** It is the issuance profile's open `mission` claim object (`id`, `origin`, `authority_hash`) with status members added (`state`, `expires_at`, and — on the dedicated response — `policy_version`, `mission_expiry`, `version`). Used as the token claim, the introspection `mission` member, and the core of the dedicated Status Response. The introspection projection's `mission` is now a clean **subset** of the dedicated response's.

**Why this direction:** the projection can't flatten (it rides inside an RFC 7662 response and must match the MVP's `mission` member), so the dedicated response — which was the outlier, and internally inconsistent (it nested `id`/`origin` but not `state`) — is the one that moves.

**Key placements:**
- `authorization_details` **stays top-level** — it's an RFC 9396 construct, a sibling of the `mission` claim on the token and in introspection; nesting it would create a *new* divergence.
- `expires_at` → into `mission` (report-freshness travels with `state`; the projection has no signed envelope to carry it). `mission-status-caching` and the replay section now reference `mission.expires_at`.
- `issued_at` **dropped** as redundant with the JWT `iat`; added a half-sentence distinguishing `exp` (signature validity) from `mission.expires_at` (state-reliance window).
- The apologetic "shapes differ deliberately" note is replaced with the consistency statement.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Add Mission Lifecycle Signals draft; define the event_driven class
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the dangling `event_driven` enforcement class in the Status profile by **defining the mechanism** rather than cutting the capability.

## New draft: `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-signals` ("Mission Lifecycle Signals for OAuth 2.0")

An OPTIONAL companion. When a Mission Issuer commits a lifecycle transition (revoke/expiry/suspend/complete, or the activating approval event), it emits a signed `mission.lifecycle-change` **Security Event Token** over a profile of the OpenID Shared Signals Framework — pushed (RFC 8935) or polled (RFC 8936). A consumer that receives a non-`active` transition stops honoring the Mission. Defines: the event stream + delivery modes, the event schema, SET protection (signed JWS, `iss`/`aud`/`jti` checks, version-ordering), consumer-on-receipt behavior, AS metadata (`mission_event_stream_endpoint`, `mission_event_delivery_modes_supported`), Security/Privacy/Conformance/IANA.

## Design decisions (per review)

- **Reuses the author's existing `mission.lifecycle-change` event-type URI**, but **registers it self-contained here** — an implementable-baseline draft must not depend normatively on the deferred, substrate-neutral MAS sketch (which "MAY change substantially"). A note records that the two are intended as one shared definition and reconcile when both advance.
- **`tenant` is OPTIONAL** in the schema so the *one* event type is shared, unchanged, with multi-tenant/cross-substrate deployments; the OAuth profile defines no tenant model (confirmed: 0 `tenant` references in the OAuth drafts).
- **`state` enum + `version` track the Status profile's current state space** (`active`/`revoked`/`expired`/`suspended`/`completed`) — not pre-empting the floated suspend/complete simplification.
- Reference entries copied verbatim from the MAS draft; subject modeling (`mission`/`mission_origin`) matches it exactly.

## Status profile

`event_driven` now reads: "the Mission Issuer emits Mission lifecycle Security Event Tokens … as defined in [signals draft]" — resolving to a real cross-reference (informative reference added). Wired the new draft into `.gitignore` + README.

Both drafts build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

---
One open question for later (not this PR): whether the canonical home for `mission.lifecycle-change` should eventually be the framework rather than this OAuth draft — relevant when the MAS reconciliation comes due.

pulls.title: Drop the redundant revocation-enforcement-class taxonomy
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
`mission_enforcement_classes_supported` was a second, parallel advertisement of capabilities already discoverable from their own metadata, so it carried no new information:

| Class | Already advertised by |
|---|---|
| `issuance` | non-optional baseline (always present) |
| `introspection` | `introspection_endpoint` + `introspection_signing_alg_values_supported` |
| `per_request` | `mission_status_endpoint` |
| `event_driven` | `mission_event_stream_endpoint` (signals draft) |

It also conflated *Resource Server* behavior with *AS* metadata, and overlapped the suite's separate capability-model vocabulary.

## Status profile
- Replaced the four-class enum + `mission_enforcement_classes_supported` with a **"Revocation Propagation"** section that points at the per-mechanism metadata and keeps the one non-redundant member, **`mission_max_stale_seconds`** (the actual revocation-latency bound).
- Removed the metadata member, its IANA registration, and the example / conformance / intro / DoS references to the dropped class names.

## Signals profile
- Reframed "Realizing the `event_driven` Enforcement Class" → **"Relationship to Revocation Propagation"**: the event stream *is* the mechanism, discovered via `mission_event_stream_endpoint` — no longer depending on the dropped taxonomy.

**No capability is lost** — every propagation mechanism stays discoverable from its own metadata. Both drafts build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Trim the Status profile: payload, auth metadata, caching, TTL
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Applies simplifications #3–#6, all to the Status profile. No capability lost.

- **#3 — Drop `version` and `policy_version`** from the status response and the `mission` object. Neither is load-bearing: nothing normative keys on them, `version`'s only consumer (`lifecycle_event`) was already removed, and `policy_version` is a derivation detail a status consumer doesn't need. Kept `state`, `authority_hash`, `expires_at`, `mission_expiry`. (The signals draft's event `version`, used for event ordering, is separate and untouched.)
- **#4 — Drop `mission_status_auth_methods_supported` / `mission_lifecycle_auth_methods_supported`** (+ their IANA registrations). Kept the requirement to authenticate with a sender-constrained method (mTLS/DPoP/private-key-JWT); the accepted methods are discovered from existing OAuth client-auth metadata.
- **#5 — Drop the caching stale-while-revalidate degraded-mode window.** Core rule stands: cache until `mission.expires_at`, don't use after.
- **#6 — Trim "Recommended Access-Token TTL"** to the actionable rule (TTL ≤ `mission_max_stale_seconds`), dropping the 3600/60–300s tutorial numbers.

Builds clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Runtime profile: address review feedback (editorial + read-gating clarity)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses the runtime-profile review. Builds clean, 0 broken xrefs.

## Editorial batch
- **Intro Non-goals** — "enforcement invariants, not a wire protocol" paragraph pointing to `{{deferred}}`.
- **Conformance (#1)** — named the three conformance targets + "not global to a product/AS/RS/PDP".
- **Policy-view version (#4)** — correlator; not policy content; not a portable registry.
- **Act chain (#5)** — consumes issuance-time delegation context; doesn't recompute subset; MAY add actor-sensitive Resource policy.
- **`authorization_details` (nit)** — "carried or otherwise available (e.g. introspection)".
- **AuthZEN example (#3)** — concrete request + response JSON; `parameter_digest` on the response/permit side (correcting the review sketch).
- **Confused deputy (security add)** — making explicit what permit-binding guarantees.

## Read-gating clarification (#2)
Resolves the table-vs-floor tension: per-class PDP-gate/parameter-binding apply once an action is **assigned** to a class; class assignment is deployment policy bounded by the floor and any Resource-policy minimum. An ordinary already-authorized read need not be classified consequential → the profile does **not** require every GET to reach a PDP. Writes and the floor classes stay MUST-gated; classification MUST NOT be used to evade the floor.

## Verified, not changed
`{#authzen}` anchor already explicit; `I-D.` and `RFC3339` keys resolve (explicit reference block, builds clean); "bounds and outlives" mirrors the issuance profile.

## Still held (separate)
**#6 evidence tiering** — restructure only, pending confirmation of the section layout.

pulls.title: Remove my em-dash asides from merged drafts
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Style cleanup: rewrite the dash-aside constructions I introduced this session, per the no-em-dash preference.

- **Signals draft** (authored this session): all 3 asides rewritten with parentheses / a sentence split.
- **Status draft**: the 2 asides in text I added (the signing-relaxation paragraph and the revocation-propagation list) rewritten. The author's own pre-existing dash usage (6 occurrences from the original import) is left untouched.

No normative change. Both drafts build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references.

pulls.title: Address MVP review: WG/security-review clarifications
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Additive clarifications from the MVP review, hardening the areas most likely to draw OAuth WG / security-review discussion. No normative change; builds clean, 0 broken xrefs.

- **Why a New Object** — make the RAR-vs-Mission distinction impossible to miss: RAR expresses authority; a Mission expresses an approved task with a lifecycle; a Mission is *not* another `authorization_details` type.
- **Mission immutable vs stateful** — clarify the record is immutable except for `state`, and that operational issuance bookkeeping (the derivation count) is AS-side state, not a member of the immutable record.
- **Two commitments** — explain why `authority_hash` and `intent_hash` are separate (authority must be verifiable from a token carrying authority alone; the task is audit material) rather than one Mission hash.
- **Derivation variability** — state that different ASes MAY derive different Authority Sets from the same Intent; the profile fixes commitment/carriage/gating, not a portable derivation algorithm.
- **Cross-domain refresh-token mode** — strengthen the rationale (one Mission, full authority, no ambiguity) and note the single-domain core needs no refresh token.
- **Introspection** — surface the RFC 7662 deviation (reporting `mission.state` for an inactive token) in the section opening.
- **Security add** — "Authority Hash Is Not a Mission Identifier": `authority_hash` commits approved authority, not the Mission; not globally unique (successor/identical-authority Missions share it); MUST NOT be used as a Mission id or replay key.

**Not changed:** the sub-matcher portability concern is already handled by the draft's existing namespace-locality + `sub_profile` guidance (the reviewer noted it's "not fatal for MVP").

Note: the reviewer's "intent_hash" matches the draft's current term (there is no `proposal_hash`).

pulls.title: Add Mission Expansion draft (optional, self-contained)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
## What

Adds `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission-expansion.md` — **Mission Expansion for OAuth 2.0**, an OPTIONAL, layered extension to the issuance profile that defines the successor-Mission mechanism the issuance draft explicitly defers ("widening authority requires a new approval, a successor Mission").

## Design

- **Successor via the issuance flow.** Expansion is a new Mission Intent submitted via PAR → a fresh approval event with its own `intent_hash`/`authority_hash`/Mission record. No new endpoint, no AuthZEN. The successor's authority comes **only** from its own consent.
- **Predecessor binding at PAR.** The request carries `mission_predecessor` (the predecessor `mission_id`, for cross-check/audit) and `mission_predecessor_token` (the predecessor's refresh token, the authoritative grant proof). The Mission Issuer resolves the predecessor from the grant — never from the identifier — and verifies they match (`invalid_grant` on mismatch). Binding before the approval event means a client cannot drive consent prompts against a Mission it does not hold a grant for.
- **`predecessor` lineage member** on the successor's `mission` claim and Mission record — lineage/audit only, MUST NOT widen authority.
- **`superseded` predecessor state** — terminal, entered atomically with successor activation; no further derivation; already-issued predecessor tokens bounded by their own `exp` (issuance revocation model); no implicit rollback.
- **Replacement is the mode; branch deferred.**
- **Concurrent reconciliation** — origin AS serializes adjudications per predecessor with compare-and-set; closed status set (`superseded_by_concurrent_expansion`, `predecessor_state_changed`).
- **Denial reasons** — closed set (`out_of_policy`, `approver_rejected`, `out_of_scope_for_purpose`).

## Scope

- Normative dependency: **only** `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission` (the issuance profile). References the runtime draft informatively as one source of an expansion-eligible denial. No dependency on the substrate-neutral suite.
- OPTIONAL throughout: a deployment that never expands a Mission is unaffected and issues no expansion request, records no `predecessor`, never enters `superseded`.

## Known-open

The `predecessor` member uses a short, unprefixed name against the issuance profile's "extension member MUST use a collision-resistant name" rule; final member naming is left for IETF expert review.

Builds clean: 0 errors, 0 unused refs, 0 long lines.

pulls.title: Resolve suite end-to-end review findings (all four drafts)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the findings from the suite-wide review across all four drafts. All four build clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc), 0 broken cross-references. Per-draft commits.

## MAJOR
- **signals `version`** — was attributed to "Mission Status," which defines no such field; the anti-masking/idempotency rules rested on it. Now defined locally as an Issuer-maintained strictly-monotonic per-Mission counter.
- **status JWS `typ`** — used the full `application/...` media type; now the short form `mission-status-response+jwt` per RFC 7515 §4.1.9 (full form kept as Content-Type + IANA registration).

## Cross-cutting
- **RFC 7662** classification unified to normative across issuance / runtime / status (status didn't cite it at all despite extending RFC 7662 introspection).
- **C3 suspend/complete** — the issuance Non-Goal now points at the Status profile (which defines the OPTIONAL `suspended`/`resume`/`complete`) instead of calling suspension future work.
- **`mission` field** — signals renamed its bare-string `mission` event field to `mission_id` (`mission` is an object elsewhere).
- **Style** — removed the 2 literal em-dashes (issuance) and the remaining `--` dash-asides (status).

## issuance (MVP)
- Resolved the `mission_id` client-access contradiction ("not expected to parse" applies to `authorization_details`; the client does read the `mission` claim id).
- RFC 9728 → normative (defines REQUIRED resource-member value spaces).
- `max_calls[].scope` → `call_class` (avoids OAuth `scope` collision; also updated in runtime).
- Added a **Privacy Considerations** section (moved Mission Identifier Correlation into it).
- Noted the normative dependencies on in-progress individual drafts (both confined to OPTIONAL capabilities).

## runtime
- AuthZEN example: `resource.type` now a resource-kind (`mission_resource`), not the RAR `authorization_details` type, with a mapping note.
- Evidence `intent_hash`: stated its only runtime source (direct Mission-record access; not in the claim or introspection).
- "Time" decision bullet de-circularized (decision-time expiry vs PEP execute-within-window reverification).
- Abstract long sentence split.

## status
- `sub` defined uniformly as the authenticated client; Lifecycle response shape spelled out (`aud` = requester; `authorization_details` MAY be omitted).
- Caching defined when `expires_at` is absent (MUST NOT cache).
- Error table clarifies 401 (no auth) vs the anti-oracle 404.

## signals (beyond `version`)
- CAEP claim softened to "uses the SSF SET shape, informed by CAEP"; dropped the unused subject-identifiers ref.
- Delivery uses the SSF method URIs (`urn:ietf:rfc:8935/8936`) discovered from SSF config; dropped the bespoke `ssf_push`/`ssf_poll` and the parallel metadata member.
- Added `exp` to the SET so the `jti` replay rule is implementable; added a resume-on-`active` consumer rule; concrete `tenant`/shared-URI reconciliation rules; TLS 1.2+/RFC 9325 floor; removed orphaned normative refs.

A couple of NITs were intentionally left (runtime permit-window terminology variants; the issuance Protocol Flow ASCII alignment) as low-value/cosmetic.

pulls.title: Resolve remaining review findings (NIT/MINOR)

Clears the MINOR/NIT findings left after PR #29. All four drafts build clean, 0 broken xrefs.

**issuance**
- Conformance promoted to a top-level section. (Conventions and Terminology kept under Introduction, matching RFC 9396 Section 1.1 — the reviewer's 'promote both' was correct for Conformance but not for Conventions.)

**signals**

Deliberately not done: the runtime 'permit lifetime control' (the bounding mechanism) is kept distinct from 'permit validity window' (the window) on purpose.

pulls.title: Bring new companion drafts to suite quality; reconcile issuance forward-refs
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
## Summary

Quality pass bringing the five newest companion drafts up to the fidelity of the mature suite, plus a reconciliation of the flagship issuance profile so its "deferred to future work" language now forward-references the companions that realize it.

**Note:** this touches the flagship `draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission` (issuance profile), not only the five new drafts — please review those edits deliberately. Build is clean across all 11 drafts with 0 broken cross-references.

## Per-draft changes

**shaping** — Mission Intent construction now emits only real Intent fields; actions/structured-constraints/delegation moved to Shaping Evidence; Title Case headings; "Conventions and Terminology"; em-dashes removed; 43-char `input_digest`; softened unobservable shaper-internal MUSTs.

**harness** — Harness Evidence aligned to the nested `mission {id,origin,authority_hash}`; added `actor`, `prior_harness_state`/`resulting_harness_state`, `state_source` enum; event_type/decision orthogonality note; "This section is OPTIONAL." on the cache; full canonical example ids; "runtime enforcement evidence" terminology.

**orchestration** — informative refs for authzen/expansion/signals; reversibility classes tied to runtime classification; Decision/Execution Evidence attributed to authzen; nested `mission` object; `state_source` aligned to the harness enum; `{#compensation}`/`{#relationship}` anchors; deployment-defined audit horizon.

**child-delegation** — Child-vs-act-chain distinction; denial reasons mapped to OAuth error codes; reframed Attenuation Rules; subset rule references issuance; cascade `superseded` re-bind to successor; IANA now registers `parent`/`parent_token`/`child_actor` in the OAuth Parameters registry; added "Status: An OPTIONAL Extension" and "Relationship to the Issuance Profile"; cite RFC8259/RFC8126.

**consent-evidence** — softened the "closes that gap" overclaim to "narrows", added an explicit disclaimer that the profile commits the structured disclosure the AS *records*, not the pixels presented or Approver comprehension; aligned abstract, Evidence Model, and Rendering Confusion to that framing; cite RFC6234/RFC8785 at `consent_rendering_hash` with algorithm-agile prefixed encoding (on the wire too); explained the source_hashes-vs-mission container asymmetry.

**issuance profile (reconciliation)** — replaced stale "deferred to future work" text with forward references: `consent_rendering_hash` → consent-evidence; mission expansion → expansion; canonical/cross-domain status + events → status + signals. Each companion kept clearly OPTIONAL; the mandatory single-domain core stays independent. Substrate-neutral model and Transaction-Token carriage remain genuine future work. Added expansion + consent-evidence as informative references.

## Verification
- `make` builds all 11 drafts clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc OK)
- 0 broken cross-references (`Section ??` / `[??]`) in every `.txt`
- no em-dashes introduced
- forward-ref claims spot-checked against the cited drafts

pulls.title: Resolve all suite-review findings (3 blocker, 17 major, 18 minor)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves all 38 findings from the end-to-end suite review (3 blocker, 17 major, 18 minor/editorial), across all 11 drafts. Ten commits, grouped by theme so the safe bulk reads fast and the real decisions stand out. All 11 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes/dash-asides.

## ⚠️ Decisions you should review deliberately (not churn)

**Four forks I asked about — all resolved the conservative way:**
1. **AuthZEN acr/amr** → reframe & drop: removed `amr_insufficient`; recast `acr_insufficient` as a single `step_up_required` Resource-policy signal (the core's `acr` is approval-time on the Approver, not a per-action constraint). *This removes a denial reason.*
2. **harness/orchestration** → de-dupe, keep two drafts (no merge). Full merge still available if you'd prefer it.
3. **IANA registries** → none. Preserved the suite's existing "no registry" stance; the collision/extension risk is met by a core forward-compat rule + collision-resistant `typ` naming instead.
4. **predecessor vs parent lineage shape** → left as-is, documented (no wire-format change to expansion).

## New normative text in the flagship core (`draft-mcguinness-oauth-mission`)

- **Lifecycle forward-compatibility rule** (load-bearing): the core now owns the lifecycle state space; wherever a state is reported, only `active` permits reliance and every other value — recognized or not — is non-active; a consumer MUST NOT fail open on an unknown state. This is what makes companion states (`suspended`/`completed`, `superseded`, `cascaded`) fail safe and dissolves the consumer side of two blockers.
- **`typ` collision-resistant-naming MUST** for new integrity anchors.
- **Mission record declared open** to companion-defined members; introspection enum tied to the extensible space.
- **Evidence-descriptor convention**: a committed evidence object MUST use the `{typ,iss,value}` envelope; an embedded `mission` descriptor is the claim shape, never authority-bearing.
- **Subset rule** now bounds a derived entry's `delegation` (no broader `max_depth`/`allowed_delegates`).
- **`max_calls.call_class`** tied to the `actions` namespace.

## Behavior changes worth conscious sign-off

- **Child Missions hard-stop on parent expansion** (B2): a `superseded` parent is now a *terminal* cascade trigger; children are no longer silently re-bound to the successor (that broke strict subset, since the successor's authority is freshly derived). Continuing requires explicit child re-creation under the successor, which re-runs the subset check. New terminal `cascaded` child state; reversible (`suspended`) vs terminal triggers distinguished, with resume restoration.
- **Runtime owns capability-source-drift**: the drift check moved from AuthZEN into runtime (out of its deferred list) as a PDP MUST where a `source_digest` was recorded at derivation; AuthZEN now only carries the refusal.
- **Read-parameter binding floor** in runtime: cross-tenant/bulk/field-selection reads MUST bind (no longer purely a "materially affects" judgment).

## Blockers (verified against source)

- **signals** could not carry `superseded` — enum reframed to the core's authoritative space + forward-compat rule; supersede emission specified.
- **child re-bind** broke strict subset + parent-member immutability — resolved as above.
- **consent-evidence** declined event couldn't populate its REQUIRED `mission.id` — made the `mission` descriptor decision-dependent (`source_hashes`-shaped, no `id`, when declined); verifier anchor-match step scoped to `approved`.

## Majors & minors (highlights)

Example integrity-anchor digests regenerated to the correct 43 chars (4 drafts); status gained a legal-transition matrix + 409 conflict handling and `superseded`/`cascaded` in its enum/terminated grouping; under-classified expansion references reconciled via the forward-compat framing; `shaping_evidence_hash` now uses the envelope; `input_digest` made recomputable; unfalsifiable shaper MUST recast around the observable evidence artifact; signals tenant "intersection" guarantee dropped for the sound consumer-robustness rules; child-delegation gained a `mission_child_delegation_supported` discovery member; AuthZEN `denial_reason` split from the open constraint-key space; em-dashes removed; redundant RFC2119/8174 declarations dropped; conventions headings standardized.

## Verification
- `make` builds all 11 drafts clean (kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc)
- 0 broken cross-references in every `.txt`
- 0 em-dashes / dash-asides
- orphan-reference sweep clean (removed `amr_insufficient`/`acr_insufficient`, old re-bind language, `intersection` framing, mixed `denial_reason` values all confirmed gone)

pulls.title: README: problem statement, mission, diagram, and per-document summaries
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Rewrites the README into a real entry point for the suite.

- **Problem statement**: OAuth issues tokens for individual requests but has no durable, approved artifact for the larger task an agent pursues.
- **The mission**: the Mission as a *governance* object (the approved task with a lifecycle that authority is derived for, bound to, and gated on), not a new way to express authority. Wording taken from the core draft's own framing.
- **ASCII diagram**: issuance core, runtime enforcement, and the OPTIONAL companion profiles grouped by function (lifecycle / sub-agents / agent runtime), plus the forward-compatibility rule.
- **Per-document summaries**: a short, fact-checked summary for each of the 11 drafts, grouped. Adds the **authzen** and **expansion** sections that were missing from the document list.

Every summary was checked against the source draft (categories, runtime's wire-format-independence, status operations, SSF/SET mechanics, expansion-vs-step-up, the consent honesty boundary, etc.). `regenerate: off` so i-d-template won't overwrite the hand-written content.

No drafts changed; README only.

pulls.title: Disambiguate the core ("issuance profile") from the suite name
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the ambiguity between the core document and the suite, both named "Mission-Bound Authorization for OAuth 2.0," and the undefined "issuance profile" nickname. Chosen approach: anchor the term in prose, keep all titles (RFC-idiomatic, mirrors how "OAuth 2.0" is both RFC 6749 and the ecosystem).

**What changed**
- **Companions define the nickname on first use.** child-delegation, consent-evidence, signals, and orchestration now introduce the core as `Mission-Bound Authorization for OAuth 2.0 {{ref}} (the "issuance profile")`, matching the five drafts that already did (authzen, expansion, runtime, status, shaping). harness uses the full title only and needs no nickname.
- **README anchors the suite-level vocabulary.** Names the family "the Mission-Bound Authorization suite," and explains that companions call the core the "issuance profile" and that the core shares its title with the suite it anchors.

**What deliberately did NOT change**
- **The core stands on its own.** It is not framed as part of a suite and does not reference the companion family; the nickname is anchored by the companions and the README, never by the core. (Dependency direction: companions reference the core, not vice versa.)
- No title or filename changes.

All edited drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Remove cross-reference citations from abstracts; cite lineage states inline
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Finishes applying a suite-wide convention: **no cross-reference citations in abstracts** (RFC 7322 — an abstract should be self-contained). These three were the last drafts that still carried `{{I-D...}}` citations in their abstract; with this change all 11 abstracts are citation-free.

- **authzen / signals / status**: drop the `{{I-D...}}` citations from the abstract and name the companions in prose instead. Every removed reference is still used in the body, so nothing is orphaned.
- **signals**: reword "registered in {{iana}}" to "defined in this document and described in {{iana}}", matching the no-new-registry stance for the author-controlled event-type URI; and clearer abstract phrasing ("builds on Mission-Bound Authorization for OAuth 2.0, the issuance profile").
- **status**: cite the defining drafts inline where `superseded` and `cascaded` appear (expansion and child-delegation).

All three build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes; no orphaned references.

pulls.title: Mediated execution + action-bound approval: defend against a compromised agent
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses the design critique that the suite *"defends against everyone except the agent"* — the agent proposes its own Mission Intent, holds the derived tokens, and runs the harness, so within a token's lifetime a compromised/injected agent wields the full envelope.

You cannot make a compromised principal trustworthy. The fix is to **stop handing the agent the dangerous authority**: make the envelope it unilaterally holds small and reversible, and put the high-consequence subset behind components it cannot compromise.

**Folded into existing drafts — no new draft** (consistent with the over-decomposition critique), and **the core is untouched** (it stands alone; runtime stays OPTIONAL).

### What changed
- **runtime — Credential custody and mediated execution** (`#custody`): a PEP placement where, for the action classes a deployment mediates, the token's *existing* sender-constraint (`cnf`) private key is held by the PEP at the last controllable boundary, not by the agent. The agent can't present the credential directly; it must act through the PEP. **No new token type, handle, or broker protocol** — purely a custody property of the existing sender-constraint key. `SHOULD` for external-commitment / privileged-administration.
- **runtime — Action-bound approval** (`#action-approval`): replaces the old "this profile does not define such a signal." A fresh approval bound to the concrete action parameters, from an independent Approver (never self-issued by the agent), reusing the issuance approval event + consent-evidence. `MUST` honor when policy requires; `SHOULD` for the top tier; TOCTOU-reverified.
- **harness — Mediated Execution Environment** (`#mediated-egress`): for mediated classes, governed work `MUST` run with no unmediated path to the resource (no debug shell, direct socket, side connector). The complement to custody — which is moot if the agent can reach the resource off-path.
- **authzen**: `action_approval_required` denial reason, carrying runtime's approval on the wire by reference.

### Calibrate the normative weight
This is the **high-assurance tier**, and it deepens exactly the deployment cost the adoption critique flagged: mediated custody + sandboxed egress + per-action approval is a platform investment, not a light touch. I kept the defaults conservative (`SHOULD` for custody and the approval requirement; `MUST` only where scoped to what a deployment already claims to mediate) so you can dial them up deliberately rather than discover the cost in the diff.

### What it still does not fix (honest residue)
A compromised PEP is game-over (disclosed); a compromised agent can still misuse low-consequence authority it legitimately holds directly; and "is *this action* faithful to the task" remains unanswerable. The goal is that the agent is *structurally unable to take a high-consequence action unilaterally*, not that it is trusted.

pulls.title: Address the up-front-bounding tension (critique #3): constraint-first, per-task, progressive authorization
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Critique #3 was that the model assumes authority can be tightly bounded at approval time, forcing a choice between a **broad standing grant** and **constant re-approval**. Three composing levers, all **folded into existing drafts** (no new draft); the core edits are guidance about its own authority model, not suite framing, so the core still stands alone.

### Lever 1 — bound by constraint, not enumeration
For an open-ended task whose concrete objects aren't known at approval ("reconcile this customer's ledger"), bound authority by **constraints that hold as invariants** (owning customer, tenant, amount ceiling, read-only-except-named-writes, time window), enforced per object by the runtime layer — rather than enumerating objects you can't know yet. Guidance added to the core's *Mission Authority* and to *shaping*.

### Lever 2 — per-task Mission granularity
A Mission **SHOULD be scoped to one concrete task**, not an agent's whole lifetime; prefer many narrow per-task Missions over one broad standing one, so the durable object stays meaningful and a compromise blast radius is one task. Guidance added to the core's *Applicability* and to *shaping*.

### Lever 3 — progressive authorization (the real mechanism)
New section in **expansion**. The initial approval MAY consent to an **authority ceiling** + a **drawdown policy**; **in-ceiling expansions are then policy-adjudicated** rather than freshly human-approved, reusing child-delegation's "policy may approve within pre-consented bounds" precedent. Key properties:
- Authority never widens without consent — the consent is the human consent to the ceiling + policy (reconciles with expansion's "authority comes only from new consent").
- Exceeding the ceiling needs a fresh human approval (new `out_of_ceiling` denial reason).
- The **active** authority any single Mission yields stays narrow even though the ceiling is broad; each in-ceiling successor is derived for what's needed at that step, gated, and revocable.
- Preserves the core invariants: each successor is its own immutable Mission with its own `authority_hash` (no mutable active set).

The core's "just-in-time expansion" Non-Goal is reworded: *approval-free* widening stays out of scope, but policy-given consent within a pre-consented, human-approved ceiling is not approval-free.

**Honest limit:** the ceiling is still broad by construction. The win is that the active authority is narrow and each drawdown is governed/rate-limitable/enforced — a bound, not an elimination — and it composes with the mediated-execution work from #37.

pulls.title: Wire ARAP as the demand-driven path to authorization growth (critique #3)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Stacked on #38. Completes the #3 answer by adding the **unanticipated-growth** half: where progressive authorization (#38) handles growth a deployment could express as a ceiling, this wires the **AuthZEN Access Request and Approval Profile (ARAP)** as the runtime-initiated, request-on-the-wall path, and connects an approved request back to expansion so the grant persists.

Grounded in the ARAP spec (requestable denial → access request + denial binding → portable task handle → re-evaluate; "approval is input context, not a bearer grant; the PDP remains authoritative").

### authzen
- `out_of_authority` and `action_approval_required` are now first-class **requestable denials**: the PDP MAY carry `context.access_request`; the PEP submits an ARAP request bound to the denied evaluation and re-evaluates on approval. The **#37 action-bound approval is exactly such an approval**, carried via ARAP's `approval.id` / signed `approval.state`.
- An approved request MAY be **realized as a Mission expansion** for persistence (in-ceiling → policy-adjudicated; beyond-ceiling → the fresh human approval the request carries).

### expansion
- New *Realizing an approved access request* (`#arap-feedback`): the durable counterpart to the single runtime permit, subject to every expansion rule (fresh derivation, supersede, no widening without the carried consent).

### runtime
- The action-bound approval MAY be obtained via the binding's request-approval workflow (AuthZEN/ARAP); approval is decision input, persistence is a Mission expansion.
- A deny is terminal but need not end the task; removed the now-specified escalation workflow from the deferred/out-of-scope list.

### The complete #3 picture (with #38)
|  | Anticipated | Unanticipated |
|---|---|---|
| Issuance (durable) | progressive authorization (ceiling + drawdown) | ARAP approval → expansion |
| Runtime (per-action) | action-bound approval (#37) | ARAP requestable denial → re-evaluate |

**Honest limits:** ARAP doesn't make the approval *decision* good (auto-approve can rubber-stamp), and it adds a runtime round-trip plus the Access Request Service to the TCB. It composes with, but doesn't replace, the mediated-execution custody from #37.

pulls.title: Make derivation interoperable where it can be (critique #4)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Critique #4: the Intent→Authority-Set derivation is non-portable local policy, so interop is only "the hashes match what this AS decided." The fix isn't to standardize the undecidable derivation *function* — it's to separate two things the critique conflates:

- **Derivation portability** (should any AS derive the same authority?) — correctly **local**, exactly like every RAR request and scope grant in OAuth; not a goal.
- **Authority-Set interoperability** (can any consumer interpret/enforce/audit the *derived* Set?) — the real, achievable goal, and where the gaps were.

All in core (*Mission Authority*, cross-domain validation, Scope) + shaping; no new draft.

### Lever A — interoperable to *enforce*
Reframe: what's interoperable is the Authority Set's structure + vocabulary + integrity anchors; a consumer enforces the **Set, not the Intent**. The `actions` member now states the understand-or-fail-closed rule (an unrecognized action is fail-closed by construction, like a scope), and a cross-domain Resource AS **MUST fail closed** on conveyed `actions`/`constraints` vocabulary it doesn't recognize rather than enforce by guess (constraints already had this).

### Lever B — reproducible + inspectable
Derivation **MUST be reproducible** (same Intent + same `policy_version` → same Authority Set), and the AS **SHOULD** make the policy behind a `policy_version` inspectable to relying parties/auditors. Local policy becomes versioned and reviewable — what a cross-domain trust decision actually needs — without the policy having to travel.

### Lever C — narrowing over generation
Where interop matters, the AS **SHOULD** derive by **narrowing a client-proposed `authorization_details`** under the standardized **subset rule** rather than generating from free text; shaping guidance has the shaper record the concrete candidate authority in Shaping Evidence for that path. Narrowing is the portable derivation path (proposal format + subset rule are both interoperable); generation is permitted but least portable.

### Lever D — named, not built
A **vertical derivation profile** (task-type → authority-template registry) is named as the future-work vehicle for communities that genuinely need portable derivation.

### Honest limits
The *semantic* question — "is this derived authority faithful to the task the user meant?" — stays local and partly AI-hard (the consent-evidence / rendering boundary, critique #5). Full algorithmic portability is neither achievable nor desirable.

Core + shaping build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add a rendering-assurance ladder (what-you-see-is-what-you-sign, critique #5)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Critique #5: "the user approved THIS task" rests on trusting the rendering layer, which no server-side commitment can prove. That's **true and stays honest** — but it's the **What-You-See-Is-What-You-Sign (WYSIWYS)** problem, which has an established assurance ladder (FIDO/WebAuthn transaction confirmation, qualified e-signatures). The suite was on the bottom rung; this builds the ladder, in consent-evidence (its home), no new draft.

The trust today is binary (trust any rendering layer). The ladder makes it tiered, each rung shrinking the trusted base and stating what it proves:

- **Rung 0 — recorded disclosure** (today): AS recorded *this* disclosure for *this* authority. Proves nothing about what was shown.
- **Rung 1 — deterministic rendering**: rendering MUST be a deterministic function of the disclosure object + committed template/locale, so an auditor can **re-render the intended form**. Narrows the gap to "did the renderer execute a published template."
- **Rung 2 — attested rendering**: optional `rendering_attestation` binds an attested/first-party/TEE renderer. Trusted base: attested renderer, not any renderer.
- **Rung 3 (headline) — Approver confirmation**: optional `rendering_confirmation`, a signature by the **Approver's authenticator over `consent_rendering_hash`**, binding the approval credential to the exact committed disclosure. "The Approver approved this disclosure" then rests on **their authenticator, not the AS**. `SHOULD` for high-risk material-notice classes. Verifier MUST check it (integrity step 6).
- **Rung 4 — out-of-band**: confirmation via the runtime action-bound approval (#37/#40) on a channel the renderer doesn't control.

### What it buys
At Rung 3 you get a **verifiable** "user approved this," bounded by trust in the authenticator — a far smaller, attestable TCB than "any rendering layer." That's an achievable form of the top-value guarantee, not a concession.

### Honest residue (kept)
No rung proves *perception*: a compromised authenticator/TEE, or a user who confirms without reading, is out of reach — true of every e-signature scheme. The ladder reduces the rendering trust base by degree; it doesn't eliminate it.

consent-evidence builds clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Resolve end-to-end review findings
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Addresses the six end-to-end findings. Prose kept tight per spec style.

**High — example reads as token-only.** The core's end-to-end example is now labeled the **baseline issuance path**, and the stages note where the layers attach: Stage 4 gains a runtime point-of-use PDP check for the consequential `journal-entries.write`; Stage 3 notes that prompt cross-domain revocation is opt-in via Status/Signals (baseline is bounded stale authority, not prompt revocation).

**Medium — cross-domain audit artifact.** Stage 3 now states what proves the Resource AS local token didn't widen beyond the ID-JAG, without recomputing `authority_hash`: the Resource AS SHOULD record, per minted token, the consumed ID-JAG `jti` and conveyed `authorization_details`, so an auditor can check the subset relation.

**Medium — cross-domain delegation limitation.** Made explicit that a sub-agent acting in a *different* trust domain under its own narrowed authority is not covered by a single hop; distributed multi-agent work composes only via the agent's projected authority or separate Missions per domain; delegate-carries-own-authority is future work.

**Medium/Low — no end-to-end bundle.** Added a concise **Recommended deployment bundles** matrix to the README (baseline issuance / enforced agent / governed agent), with **consent-evidence in the recommended AI-agent bundle**, since per-draft scoped conformance otherwise lets an issuance-only deployment miss action-time checks, stop, unwind, and consent evidence.

Also adds runtime as an informative reference in the core (the example uses it) — which also closes the architecture-review note that the core was silent on runtime.

pulls.title: Resolve fresh end-to-end review: BLOCKER + progressive-auth tightening + soundness fixes
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the unambiguous findings from the fresh end-to-end review and tightens progressive authorization (the sharpest edge the security reviewer found). Prose kept tight.

### BLOCKER
- **expansion**: the progressive-authorization ceiling/drawdown policy were said to be "covered by `authority_hash`". `authority_hash` commits only the consented Authority Set. Now committed under their own anchor/record member; `authority_hash` stays the active set.

### Progressive authorization tightened (strongest-attack fix: "ceiling as standing grant")
- **expansion**: an in-ceiling drawdown for an **irreversible / external-commitment / privileged-administration / cross-domain** class **MUST be human-approved**, not policy-only; drawdown policy **SHOULD** rate-limit; a policy-declined in-ceiling request falls back to a freshly human-approved expansion.
- **authzen**: ARAP synchronous auto-approval **MUST NOT** cover those classes without an independent approver, and **SHOULD** be rate/anomaly-checked.
- **runtime**: the "mediated classes" set is the load-bearing parameter; a deployment relying on this profile against agent compromise **MUST** mediate the high-consequence classes; added `irreversible` to the action-bound-approval SHOULD.

### Soundness fixes
- **consent-evidence**: rung-3 `rendering_confirmation` MUST sign the hash **plus a per-approval value** and bind to an **authenticator tied to the recorded `approver`** (kills replay; gives real AS-independence); added a verifier step for rung-2 `rendering_attestation`; stated the rungs are **cumulative**.
- **runtime**: clarified the mediating PEP is a co-trusted process, **not a delegate** (agent stays principal of record; no `act` entry).
- **trust-base disclosure**: ARAP Access Request Service (authzen), attestation verifier (consent-evidence), drawdown-policy engine (expansion) surfaced as trusted components.
- **orchestration**: promoted authzen to a **normative** reference (it uses the Decision/Execution Evidence schemas).
- **naming drift**: status/authzen "base profile" → issuance/runtime profile; harness imports the PEP term.
- **core abstract**: scoped the "mission layer" to issuance+governance, noting runtime enforcement is a separate optional layer — **this closes critique #1 (oversell)**.
- **README bundles**: shaping placement; "status and/or signals"; softened the "independently optional" framing.

Deferred (your call, not in this PR): the status+signals and runtime+authzen **consolidation** merges.
