pulls.title: Add Mission Deferred Approval companion (profiles gerber Deferred Token Response)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
New OPTIONAL companion: **Mission Deferred Approval**, the approval-time interaction protocol the issuance profile left unspecified. Built per your direction: a **single companion**, on **draft-gerber-oauth-deferred-token-response**, now (ahead of consolidation).

## What it adds
The core records an approval event but treats it as immediate. Real agent review is asynchronous and reviewers commonly approve a **narrowed subset**. This companion profiles gerber's Deferred Token Response (DTR) to supply both:

- **Deferred approval** — the approval MAY be deferred (`completion_mode=deferred`), polled with the DTR deferred grant type. Timing only; the committed authority and anchors are unchanged.
- **Revisable approval** — a `revisable` completion-mode value (registered in DTR's Completion Mode Values registry). When the AS can grant only a narrowed Mission, it extends DTR's `authorization_pending` response (using DTR's §5.4 add-response-parameters hook, so non-revisable clients keep polling normally) with `revision_required` + `clarification_handle` + `rejected_scope`/`rejected_authorization_details`. The client pushes a **narrowing** revision via PAR, bound by the single-use, sender-constrained handle, and keeps polling the same `deferral_code`. Narrowing enforced by the core subset rule (RFC 9396 inclusion for `authorization_details`).

## Design choices (per the decision note, included in the PR)
- **Gerber-native**: a completion-mode value + response parameters on the pending response, not a new error code or a separate grant_mode parameter — matches DTR's actual extension model.
- **Single companion** (not abstract+binding split), core untouched, OPTIONAL.
- **Scoped against the four existing narrow/widen concepts**: shaping (narrow before), derivation/subset (narrow at derivation), expansion (widen after), progressive authorization (drawdown). This is the fifth, distinct one: negotiate-the-approval, narrowing, during review.
- **Integrates** with consent-evidence (fresh `consent_rendering_hash` on re-review), shaping (the `rejected_*` signals feed re-shaping), and the integrity anchors (final narrowed `authority_hash`).

## Notes
- Normative deps on the core and gerber DTR (both individual drafts), so it cannot advance ahead of them — same posture as the suite's other normative deps.
- Wired into README (new section under a renamed "Approval time" group + an approval-time bundle note) and .gitignore.
- This is additive scope; the **status+signals / runtime+authzen consolidation** remains the separate larger pass.

All 12 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Make consent-vs-enforcement honesty explicit at the approval point
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves the review's findings 3, 4, 5 and the two smaller items as core honest-framing fixes; #6 gets a framing clarification with the category decision left to you.

- **#3 (the family's biggest footgun)** — at the approval/rendering step, the core now states plainly that *rendering a bound is not enforcing it*: per-entry `constraints` bind only at an RS that enforces the key, the consumption bounds (`max_budget`/`max_calls`/`max_duration`) only under the runtime layer, and absent those they are **advisory** (rendered, consented, committed, enforced nowhere). The AS SHOULD tell the Approver which rendered bounds its deployment actually enforces. This puts the README's "audit, not action-time defense" honesty where the Approver's consent is actually formed.
- **#4** — same place: `max_derivations` is an origin-AS cap that does **not** bound a Resource AS's local-token minting in another domain, so "max 200 derivations" is not read as an end-to-end cap.
- **#5** — the flat subset rule's limitation (no resource containment / action families; byte-exact `resource`, flat `actions`) is now named as **known near-term work in the core**, not only in runtime's out-of-scope list, since it is the subset rule's limitation.
- **smaller (authority_hash)** — the abstract now leads with "audit anchor, verifiable by full-set holders"; downstream/cross-domain parties hold only subsets and can't recompute it.
- **smaller (aud vs resource)** — reconciled the "aud derived from resource" line with the coarser-`aud` rule, so an implementer doesn't read `aud == resource`.

**#6 (runtime Standards Track)** — I made the *framing* fix only: runtime now states it is the architecture/invariant layer and the interoperable wire surface is the AuthZEN binding (two invariant-conforming deployments don't thereby interoperate at the PEP-PDP boundary). I did **not** change its category. The real options are: (a) keep Standards Track and defend the invariants-spec framing; (b) move the normative wire bits into the AuthZEN draft and make runtime Informational/BCP. That's your call — happy to do (b) as a focused change if you want it.

Core + runtime build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Strengthen the load-bearing properties (review findings 1, 2, 3, 5)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Resolves findings 1, 2, 3, and 5 as scoped fixes. **#4 (offline attenuation)** is held as a separate companion effort to green-light next, per the analysis.

### #1 — Mandatory freshness for the classes that justify it (runtime)
The kill switch was soft (latency bounded by token lifetime). Now: for the irreversible / external-commitment / privileged-administration classes, the Mission state source **MUST** be an active freshness mechanism (introspection, Status, or Signals); **token-lifetime expiry alone is not acceptable** for these classes, since it only ages out a revoked Mission instead of stopping it. Closes the "marketing says instant, mechanics say eventual" gap where it matters.

### #2 — Named agent-compromise-resistant enforcement tier (runtime)
Turns the self-asserted claim into a verifiable one. A deployment claims **agent-compromise-resistant enforcement** only when, for the high-consequence classes, custody (cnf key in the PEP), no-unmediated-egress, action-bound approval, and active freshness are **all MUST** (each is SHOULD in the base profile). Leave any as SHOULD → must not make the claim. "Protects against agent compromise" now has teeth.

### #3 — Consent binds the derived authority, not the goal (core + consent-evidence)
The deepest consent-integrity gap, closed at the principle level: the object the Approver consents to is the **derived Authority Set** rendered in human terms; `goal`/`success_criteria`/`purpose` are context; an approval surface — or a consent disclosure (`authority_summary`) — that renders only the goal and not the derived authority **does not conform**. Shuts the goal-only product default.

### #5 — Scope the derivation-reproducibility MUST (core)
Corrects a MUST I introduced that the permitted free-text path violated. Reproducibility is **MUST** for narrow-from-proposed-`authorization_details` (deterministic); generative/free-text derivation is **SHOULD/best-effort with disclosure** plus `policy_version` for after-the-fact review.

README bundle rows reflect #1/#2 non-normatively (teeth live in runtime). Core + runtime + consent-evidence build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add Mission Offline Attenuation companion (profiles Attenuating Agent Tokens)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Finding #4, green-lit: removes the Authorization Server from the sub-agent fan-out hot path. New OPTIONAL companion profiling Attenuating Agent Tokens (draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens) — a Mission-bound token holder mints a narrower child **offline**, no issuer round-trip, carrying the same `mission` claim.

I fetched the AAT substrate to profile it faithfully; the key fact shaping the design is that **AAT defines no revocation of its own**, which is exactly why the runtime-state recheck has to be mandatory here.

### Built with the three preconditions from the analysis
- **Verifiable narrowing**: the child carries the parent token chain, so a consumer checks the subset (AAT's capability monotonicity) from the tokens it holds — solving "the consumer only holds the child."
- **Kill switch via runtime**: AAT can't recall an offline child, so a Mission-bound attenuation chain **MUST** be consumed under the runtime profile, which re-checks Mission state at use; a revoked Mission stops the whole chain regardless of children's `exp`. A deployment **MUST NOT** accept these tokens on a path that doesn't enforce Mission state — so it's an **enforced-tier** capability.
- **`authority_hash` as lineage**: it commits the root Mission's Authority Set and rides the chain as an audit anchor, not the narrower child's own commitment.

### Boundaries
Offered **alongside** AS-mediated delegation (online, issuer-observed), not instead of it. Distinct from **Child Mission Delegation** (which creates a separate child Mission with its own lifecycle; this creates no new Mission — every child rides the same Mission and dies with it).

Additive companion (consistent with the no-consolidation decision); core untouched; wired into README (Sub-agents group) + .gitignore. All 13 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

Note: adds a normative dependency on draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens (an individual draft), so it can't advance ahead of it — same posture as the gerber DTR dependency in Mission Deferred Approval.

pulls.title: Name the prompt-injection threat model; add a harness session-taint rule
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Acts on the lethal-trifecta review: states the posture the design already takes implicitly, and adds the session-level control the per-action layer structurally can't express. Did both of the reviewer's offers, since they're complementary.

## Framing (honest, each draft about its own layer)

- **core security considerations** names the threat (an injected agent combining private-data access, untrusted-content exposure, and external communication). The core constrains the data-access leg (a Mission narrows authority to the task; per-task Missions shrink blast radius), contributes the inert `goal`/`purpose`/`success_criteria` so injected text can't talk a Mission into widening itself, and states plainly it provides no information-flow control: within-scope laundering is out of reach, and exfiltration is the runtime layer's job.
- **runtime security considerations** state that runtime gates the exfiltration leg (external communication is consequential; with mediated execution the agent doesn't even hold the egress credential), but conditional on complete PEP placement (the Achilles' heel: every channel, DNS, logs, error strings, shared stores, must be mediated, and it can't prove a deployment enumerated them all), and with no information-flow control (a sequence of authorized steps can compose into an exfiltration no single check catches; `max_calls`/`max_budget` bound volume, not flow).

## Mechanism (the design opportunity)

- **harness** gets a new "Untrusted Content and Egress" section. A governed session is tainted once attacker-influenceable content enters it (a fetched doc, a tool result, an inbound message). Once tainted, before a consequential external-communication or external-commitment action, the harness SHOULD require a fresh action-bound approval or downgrade that authority, the plan-then-execute pattern: untrusted content may inform planning but must not, alone, drive egress. Framed honestly as a coarse session-level control, not information-flow control (it doesn't track which datum reached which action; it raises the bar; it is recorded in Harness Evidence). This converts the trifecta defense from "hoped deployments preserve it" into something the harness enforces, and gives the harness draft a sharper purpose.

This is the honest framing a Cedar/AuthZEN reviewer will test, stated before they do. Core untouched in substance (security-considerations text only). All 13 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add Mission Audit Transparency companion (profiles SCITT)
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
New OPTIONAL companion: **Mission Audit Transparency**, profiling the SCITT architecture so the suite's audit trail becomes tamper-evident, append-only, and independently verifiable. I fetched the SCITT architecture to profile it faithfully.

## The gap
The suite produces many evidence records (approval event, lifecycle transitions, runtime decision/execution, consent, harness, orchestration, child). Each is signed, but *signed is not transparent*: a key holder can backdate, drop, or reorder records, and a cross-domain auditor must trust the issuer's own logs.

## The profile (defines no new evidence; registers the existing records)
- **Registers** Mission evidence as SCITT **Signed Statements**.
- **Mission as subject** (`sub`), derived deterministically from `mission` `origin`+`id`, so all of a Mission's evidence is one **append-only feed** an auditor replays as a single coherent narrative (the "one mission, one story" property).
- **Detached payload / commit-by-hash**: statements commit to the evidence by its existing integrity anchor, not the evidence itself, so **sensitive task data stays out of the log**; the evidence is retrieved separately under access control and checked against the commitment.
- **Receipt** retained as a **Transparent Statement**, so any party in any domain verifies inclusion **offline** — which is also a partial answer to the cross-domain provenance gap a bare narrowed-token signature can't close.

## Honest limits (stated in the draft)
Accountability, not prevention: a dishonest producer can register a false record; transparency makes it permanent, attributable, and visible — SCITT's own model. It proves a record was *registered*, not that the action occurred or was authorized. Completeness is only visible against an expected cadence. Privacy: append-only log → commit-by-hash only, and `sub` derived so it doesn't expose the Subject/task.

## Notes
Additive companion (consistent with the no-consolidation decision); core untouched; new "Audit" group in the README. Normative dependency on `draft-ietf-scitt-architecture` (a WG draft), so it can't advance ahead of it — same posture as the gerber-DTR and AAT companions. All 14 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.

pulls.title: Add worked non-normative examples across the family
pulls.author: mcguinness
pulls.body:
Reviewed all 14 drafts for worked non-normative examples. Seven had none or only an object-dump; each now has a labeled **Worked Example**, all in the suite's running scenario (agent `s6BhdRkqt3` for `alice`, Mission `msn_8RfX2Lqv9TqMv4z7sA2bN1k0YpEdHc9-`, reconcile Q3 invoices, canonical ids and 43-char anchors), so the examples read as one coherent story across the family.

| Draft | Worked example added |
|---|---|
| **attenuation** | Mission-bound AAT root, an offline-minted read-only child (iss=jwk-thumbprint, `par_hash`, narrowed tools), gateway chain + PoP + Mission-state checks |
| **audit** | SCITT Signed Statement (COSE EDN) committing a consent-evidence hash, Mission-as-`sub` feed listing, cross-domain auditor verifying the Receipt offline |
| **approval** | full deferred → `authorization_pending` → `revision_required` (drop write) → PAR narrow → poll → token flow |
| **signals** | revoke transition as a SET pushed to a partner consumer that stops within seconds; `version` defeats a stale `active` |
| **shaping** | free-text goal → constraint-first, per-task candidate Mission Intent, with the AS (not the shaper) deriving the Authority Set |
| **harness** | overnight resume that finds the Mission revoked and suppresses, plus the session-taint egress gate after an untrusted email enters the session |
| **expansion** | mid-task $1,200 adjustment → expansion request → fresh consent → superseded predecessor with a `predecessor` member |

The other seven drafts already carry at least one strong worked example and were left as-is: **core / authzen / status** are example-rich; **runtime** has the AuthZEN binding flow; **orchestration** has the Worked Unwind Plan; **child-delegation** has the parent/child token; **consent-evidence** has the full Consent Evidence object.

All 14 drafts build clean; 0 broken cross-references; 0 em-dashes.
